title: Chapter 5: The Castle at the Edge of Nowhere
content: <!-- Speed: Breather → Sprint | D3+D9 -->
Night fell somewhere past Bakersfield.
They had been driving for seven hours. Shadowfax's air conditioning had given up entirely around mile marker 218, and the car now smelled like Monster Energy, Dorito dust, and determination.
"We need to stop," Sam said. Her third iced coffee was empty. Her fourth would require a gas station, and the next gas station, according to the glowing screen of her phone, was seventeen miles away.
"The Queen does not require rest."
"The Queen's Royal Treasurer requires rest. And a bathroom. And possibly a lobotomy."
Chloe considered this. "The needs of the Royal Court must be balanced with the needs of the Quest."
"Thank you."
"We shall seek shelter at the next stronghold."
"You mean a motel."
"A fortified waystation for weary travelers."
"A motel."
"Semantics."
The Desert Rose Inn materialized out of the darkness like an oasis — if an oasis had a flickering neon sign where the "D" and the "s" had both burned out, rendering it "e ert Ro e Inn."
Chloe gasped. "The Fortress of the Faded Light. Ancient. Mysterious. Possibly haunted."
"It's a motel with a broken sign."
"The broken sign is a ward. It keeps out those who cannot see past the surface of things."
"It keeps out people with reading comprehension."

Sam parked under the only working lamp post. The parking lot had three other cars: a Prius with a coexist bumper sticker, a pickup truck that had probably voted for Trump, and a minivan covered in what appeared to be children's handprints made of Cheeto dust.
"Diverse clientele," Chloe observed. "The Fortress accepts all travelers."
"It's a motel off the I-5. It accepts anyone with a credit card."

They walked into the lobby. The man behind the counter was approximately seventy years old and looked like he had been sitting there for at least sixty of them. He was watching a reality TV show on a small CRT television with rabbit-ear antennas.
"Welcome to the Desert Rose," he said without looking up. "One bed or two?"
"Two," Sam said.
"One royal suite and one adjacent chamber," Chloe corrected.
"Two beds," Sam repeated.
The man looked up. Saw the plastic crown. Saw the foam sword. Saw Sam's expression, which was the expression of someone who had stopped being surprised eight hours ago.
"We don't have a royal suite."
"All rooms are royal suites if you adjust your perception," Chloe said.
He stared at her. Then at Sam. Then back at Chloe.
"...seventy-two dollars. Cash or card?"

Room 12 smelled like bleach and bad decisions. Two twin beds with floral comforters that had probably been floral in 1987. A TV that only played channels 3 through 7. A painting of a desert sunset that was hung slightly crooked, which Chloe immediately identified as "a hidden message from the Fortress's original architects."
"It's crooked," Sam said.
"Exactly. The architects are telling us to look at things from a different angle."
Sam straightened the painting. "Now what are they telling us?"
"That you're a conformist who can't handle subtext."
Sam flopped onto the bed closer to the door. The springs made a sound like a small animal being stepped on. She stared at the ceiling. Water stain in the shape of Florida.
"Chloe."
"Mm?"
"Why are we doing this?"
Chloe was arranging her crown on the nightstand, positioning it so it caught the lamplight. "We're reclaiming Avalon."
"No. Really. Why did I say yes?"

Chloe stopped arranging. She turned. For a moment — just a moment — the fantasy dropped. Her voice was different. Quieter. "Because when you had your last panic attack, I stayed up with you until 4 AM. And when I lost my mind in that Zoom meeting, you didn't call an ambulance. You packed my keyboard."
Sam was quiet for a long time. The TV murmured something about a house renovation in the background.
"I packed your keyboard because I knew you'd want it," Sam said finally. "The one with the RGB lighting you programmed to flash in D&D patterns."
"And that's why you're here. You're the only person in the world who knows which keyboard I'd want."
Sam's eyes were doing something. She blinked hard.
"It's a really stupid road trip," she said.
"It's a quest."
"It's a really stupid quest."
"The best quests are stupid. The smart ones are just jobs with better scenery."
Sam couldn't argue with that either.

Morning came through curtains that didn't quite close. Chloe woke first — she always did. She stood at the window, looking out at the parking lot, at the Prius and the pickup and the Cheeto-dusted minivan, and she saw a courtyard of allied kingdoms preparing for war.
Sam woke to the sound of Chloe humming the Lord of the Rings soundtrack.
"It's 7 AM."
"Dawn is the optimal time for questing. The shadows are long. The light is golden. The enemies are still sleeping."
"I'm the enemy and I'm still sleeping."

They checked out. The old man at the counter didn't ask questions. In his sixty-plus years at the Desert Rose, he had learned that the ones in costumes were usually less trouble than the ones in business suits.
Breakfast was the diner next door: "LUCKY'S 24-HOUR EATS." It had a jukebox that only played Patsy Cline and a waitress named Doris who called everyone "honey" and meant it.
"What can I get you honeys?"
"The Pancake Plate of Endurance," Chloe said, pointing at the menu.
"That's just pancakes."
"Not when you're on a quest."
Doris looked at Sam. Sam said: "Same. And coffee. A lot of coffee."
"Coming right up, honeys."

At the counter, a trucker in a John Deere cap was watching the small TV above the register. The morning news. And on the morning news, a clip: shaky phone footage of a woman in a plastic crown holding a foam sword, telling a bucket-hat-wearing influencer that his hat was a forty-dollar insecurity.
The chyron read: "MYSTERY WOMAN GOES VIRAL AFTER STRAWBERRY FARM 'EXORCISM.'"
Sam choked on her water. Chloe turned slowly.
"We're on the news," Sam whispered.
"We're folk heroes," Chloe corrected.
The trucker squinted at the screen, then at Chloe, then back at the screen.
"Hey," he said. "Are you that crazy lady from TikTok?"
Chloe straightened her crown. "I prefer 'visionary.'"
The trucker laughed — the kind of deep, genuine laugh of someone who had been on the road too long and appreciated any break in the monotony. "Visionary. I like that. What's your vision?"
"The liberation of Avalon from the forces of late-stage capitalism."
"Avalon."
"Los Angeles."
"Hell of a commute."
"It's not a commute," Chloe said. "It's a pilgrimage."
The trucker nodded slowly. He had the look of a man who had seen enough of America to know that the line between visionary and crazy was mostly a matter of who was holding the camera.
"Well," he said, "good luck with your pilgrimage. And if you hit LA, stay off the 405 during rush hour. That's not capitalism. That's just pure evil."
"Noted," Chloe said.
Doris arrived with pancakes — enormous, golden, steaming. "Here you go, honeys. Royal breakfast."
Chloe looked at Sam. Sam looked at Chloe. They both looked at Doris.
"You heard that?" Sam asked.
"Heard what, honey?"
"Royal breakfast."
Doris shrugged. "You got a crown on, sweetheart. Seems appropriate."
Chloe's face did something complicated. "The common folk recognize their queen."
"The common folk recognize a girl who needs pancakes," Doris said. "Now eat before they get cold."
Back on the road. Shadowfax coughed to life on the third try. The gas tank was at a quarter. The next stop: Los Angeles. Or Avalon. Same coordinates.
"That trucker knew who you were," Sam said. "From TikTok. We're going viral."
"Good. The people need to know their queen is coming."
"The people think you're a performance artist having a mental breakdown."
"Those are the same thing."
Sam opened her mouth to argue, then closed it. She wasn't sure they weren't.
